name: french beef and mushroom stew
notes:
French beef and mushroom stew — a bourguignon-shaped dish where beef and mushrooms are co-equal primaries, not a substitution of one for the other. REAL CHUNKS OF BEEF (chuck or similar stew cut) are the default. For a vegan version, GROUND vegan beef is the substitute — see the meat note below on why ground vegan beats chunky seitan here. The mushrooms aren't standing in for anything — they're the second headliner alongside the beef.

TECHNIQUE: start with sauteed mushrooms in butter, then flour for roux. Other vegetables like celery/carrot are stew vegetables coarsely chopped, not sauce base (finely chopped and sauteed).

COARSE CHOP + DEEP SAUTÉ (NO MIREPOIX): coarse-chop the onions, carrots, celery, and mushrooms into visible chunks — same rule as coq au vin, this is a rustic stew and you want to see and taste discrete vegetable pieces in the finished dish, not a dissolved-into-sauce mirepoix base. That said, sauté them HARD enough to get real caramelization on the surfaces before the wine and stock go in. The deep-sauté / maillard phase does structural flavor work that a rushed softening step doesn't provide. Rush the sauté and the finished stew reads flat.

CELERY IS A RESCUE INGREDIENT — SECONDARY, WITH A MEDITERRANEAN COST: celery isn't primary here, but it's an unusually useful correction step when the dish has gone off balance. If you dumped in too much tomato paste, over-poured the red wine, or otherwise let one axis dominate, adding coarsely-chopped celery to the braise pulls the flavors back toward center — its neutral savory-vegetal character absorbs excess tang and mellows the dish. THE COST: celery drags the stew's register toward mediterranean-vegetable-soup / minestrone territory, which is exactly the direction the rosemary + bay + tomato axis is trying to escape. So use celery when you need to rescue a batch, but know that a rescued stew reads slightly more italian and less french-classical than a properly-balanced one. If you're using celery from the start (rather than as a fix), keep the quantity modest — a stalk or two, coarse chop, not a full mirepoix.

POTATOES ARE CRITICAL — CANONICAL STARCHY BODY: potatoes simmering in the pot are load-bearing for this recipe. They give the stew its starchy, hearty body and absorb the tomato-and-wine sauce properly. Coarse-chop them into ~1-inch chunks and add during the braise. This is a MAJOR DIFFERENTIATOR from coq au vin (see coq-au-vin), which has NO potatoes and would be ruined by them — the beef variant's tomato + worcestershire umami structure holds up to the starch absorption; coq au vin's delicate pinot noir base would be dulled and diluted.

MEAT — REAL BEEF CHUNKS DEFAULT; GROUND VEGAN BEEF FOR THE VEGAN VERSION:
- REAL BEEF (CHUNKS): the default is real chuck (or similar stew-cut beef) cut into bite-sized chunks. The chunks carry the umami and texture the long braise needs, and the connective tissue breaks down over the cook to enrich the sauce. This is a big shift from thinking about this as a ground-beef dish — for the real-beef version, go chunks.
- GROUND VEGAN BEEF (VEGAN PATH): for the meatless version, ground vegan beef is the right substitute — Abbott's or similar neutral products. Notably, GROUND VEGAN BEEF WORKS BETTER HERE THAN CHUNKY SEITAN. Seitan chunks don't render or break down like real beef chuck does; they stay rubbery and don't contribute to the sauce. Ground vegan beef is small enough to distribute through the dish and doesn't have that same rubbery-chunk problem. If you're going vegan, go ground.
- IMPOSSIBLE / BEYOND BEEF (VEGAN PATH ALTERNATE): borderline — too distinctly flavored for this dish. If using, form into MEATBALLS (see impossible-meat note below) rather than crumbles.
- SOY CURLS: untested here (see want_to_try). Curious whether they'd behave more like real beef chunks than seitan does; my hypothesis is they'd rehydrate and pick up the braise flavor well without the seitan-rubber problem.
Flavor in the vegetarian version is NOT derived from the meat, so a good vegan beef bouillon is doing the heavy lifting.

HERBS / AROMATICS (non-optional): rosemary and bay leaf in large quantities are mandatory. Rosemary is nice-to-have in chicken stews or italian vegetable soups, but it's mandatory in this vegetarian beef stew — the wine + soy meat + potatoes + mushrooms + carrots will be flat without the sharpness of bay leaf and rosemary together. Garlic is also non-optional. Use sharp white onions, not yellow or red.

ROSEMARY IS RIGHT HERE, WRONG IN COQ AU VIN (SEE COQ-AU-VIN): the coq au vin recipe explicitly clashes rosemary — its pinot-noir-and-chicken register is too delicate and rosemary overpowers. This beef variant is the opposite: the wine is bigger (see wine note below), the meat is richer, and rosemary is what pulls it together. The two dishes are structurally opposed on this axis. Don't backport thyme+sage from coq au vin here (too subtle) and don't backport rosemary to coq au vin (too aggressive).

WINE — NOT CABERNET (SAME RULE AS COQ AU VIN): cabernet's tannins and fruit-forward jamminess can ruin this dish too, even though the beef and tomato structure is more forgiving than coq au vin's. A cabernet ends up dominating the sauce and pushing the flavor toward a red-wine-reduction rather than a stew. A traditional burgundy or pinot noir works; a syrah or malbec is fine here (unlike coq au vin) because the beef and tomato paste can hold up to a bigger red. Just not cabernet.

NORTHERN PULL: worcestershire sauce rounds it out and pushes the flavor more 'northern'. Without it the rosemary/bay/tomato combination can drift italian even with the potatoes.

TOMATO: use more tomato paste than traditional boeuf bourguignon, and add a chopped fresh tomato as extra tomato flavor — you get less umami from the pseudo meat than from real beef, so compensate here. Tomatoes are non-optional for this recipe.

LARD AND BUTTER — SECONDARY HERE, PRIMARY IN COQ AU VIN: butter is standard for the mushroom sauté and roux; lard alongside is a nice enrichment but not required. Both are secondary because the tomato paste + worcestershire + red wine + real beef fat combination is already carrying substantial umami. Contrast with coq au vin (see coq-au-vin), which has no tomato and leans much harder on lard + butter (traditionally lardons) as the umami base. If you want to lean french-classical in either direction, add lardons here as an optional secondary — they're not traditional in bourguignon, but they work.

RED CHILI FLAKES: a real ingredient in this dish, not a topping. The chili flakes pair particularly well with the tomato + worcestershire (or tamarind paste) axis — they activate the tang and amplify the depth. Without them the dish reads slightly flatter against the same ingredient set. Stir into the simmer; don't sprinkle at the end.

IMPOSSIBLE / BEYOND BEEF — COOK AS MEATBALLS, NOT CRUMBLES: if using impossible meat, beyond beef, or similar extruded plant-based ground beef, form it into MEATBALLS before adding to the stew. Don't break it into crumbles. The extruded proteins in these products recombine during baking — crumbles turn back into a monolithic slab of pseudo-meat in the pot. Pre-formed meatballs hold their shape, and the dish ends up with discrete chunks instead of one giant brick. This is unique to the soy-based products; real ground beef and abbott's-style products don't behave this way.
